insights: Finance Cost Efficiency objectives have a mean duration of 235.56 days—56% longer than the overall dataset and 2.5× longer than Finance’s other categories—indicating a department-specific operational bottleneck in executing cost-related goals.

The Q3 2022 cohort (30 objectives) drives the timeline extension most severely, with a mean duration of 289.90 days—68% longer than Q1 2022—and only 26.67% goal met rate, confirming a systemic, time-bound disruption rather than a chronic or priority-driven issue.

56.3% of Finance Cost Efficiency objectives are stuck in 'In Progress' state, yet this state shows no meaningful correlation with priority level: Low Priority In Progress objectives are actually 12.2% shorter in duration than other Low Priority states, ruling out priority misassignment as a root cause.

16 objectives (22.5% of all 'In Progress' Finance Cost Efficiency objectives) are ≥75% complete but remain uncompleted, with a mean duration of 249.50 days—representing the highest-leverage intervention point for near-term timeline compression.

Cancelled Finance Cost Efficiency objectives have the longest mean duration (259.08 days), suggesting late-stage abandonment after prolonged resource investment rather than early de-escalation, exposing governance gaps in objective viability review.

Owner fragmentation is extreme—75 unique owners across 126 objectives—with no evidence of owner-level duration patterns, indicating that accountability and ownership continuity are not contributing to delays but may hinder consistent process adherence.
summary: Finance Cost Efficiency objectives exhibit chronically extended timelines—235.56 days on average—driven not by individual owner behavior or priority misallocation, but by a systemic cohort effect centered on Q3 2022, where objectives ran 68% longer and achieved goals at less than half the rate of Q1 2022. The dominant operational pattern is a state transition bottleneck: over half of all objectives stall in 'In Progress', and a high-value subset (16 objectives) remains stuck despite being ≥75% complete. Critically, low priority assignment does not explain the delay—Q3’s extended timelines persist across all priority-state combinations—pointing to external or process-level factors introduced mid-2022. To improve objective management, immediate focus should shift from re-prioritization to strengthening state-governance protocols—especially exit criteria for 'In Progress'—and implementing proactive checkpoint reviews for objectives approaching 75% completion.
